title_en: Methodology and Structure of Tuhfat al-Muhtaj
content_en:
Tuhfat al-Muhtaj bi-Sharh al-Minhaj — The Gift to the One in Need, a Commentary on al-Minhaj — is Ibn Hajar al-Haytami's commentary on an-Nawawi's Minhaj at-Talibin, and it stands alongside Nihayat al-Muhtaj by ar-Ramli as the two supreme references of the later Shafi'i school. The two works are studied together in advanced Shafi'i education because they represent the most authoritative and detailed engagement with the Minhaj's text available in the tradition.

Ibn Hajar's methodology in the Tuhfa is characterized by meticulous attention to the language of the Minhaj, engagement with the full range of prior Shafi'i scholarship, and willingness to defend positions that had been challenged or left unsettled in the tradition. He draws extensively on an-Nawawi's Rawdat at-Talibin and al-Majmu, on the works of ar-Rafi'i, on the Mughni al-Muhtaj of al-Khatib ash-Shirbini, and on earlier Shafi'i masters such as al-Qaffal, al-Baghawi, and al-Mutawalli. His engagement with these sources is not merely citational but critical — he evaluates their positions and indicates where he agrees or disagrees.

Structurally, the Tuhfa follows the Minhaj chapter by chapter, providing a detailed expansion of each section. The work runs to ten volumes in the standard edition (with hashiyas). Ibn Hajar's prose is dense but precise, reflecting both his depth of learning and his conviction that legal language must be exact. He uses established technical vocabulary consistently and explains new usages when they arise.

A notable methodological feature is Ibn Hajar's treatment of the disagreements between ar-Rafi'i and an-Nawawi. Where these two foundational authorities of the later Shafi'i school differ, Ibn Hajar assesses the evidence and indicates which position is stronger. His assessments on these disputed points became authoritative for the Hijazi branch of the tradition, giving the Tuhfa a normative function beyond mere exposition. The comparison between the Tuhfa and Nihayat al-Muhtaj on these disputed points became a distinct scholarly genre: works cataloging the ar-Ramli vs. Ibn Hajar disagreements helped practitioners determine which authority governed in their regional context, while also training students in the identification and analysis of principled juristic disagreement within a single legal school. This genre of comparative commentary reflects the Shafi'i tradition's sophisticated approach to managing its own internal diversity.
